summary:
Obstructive sleep apnea resulting from structural alterations in the upper airway often stems from malformations such as retrognathia or mandibular hypoplasia. Bilateral TMJ ankylosis contributes by restricting mandibular movement, promoting posterior tongue displacement and narrowing of the pharynx—the most plausible choice among provided options due to its direct biomechanical linkage to airway collapse.  

The summary above contains two complete sentences focusing solely on clinically significant aspects related to the causation of obstructive sleep apnea via anatomy-related mechanisms.
